column3:
And I have always liked gambling type mini games in open world games, from poker in far cry 3 (was it poker or something else?) to the dice games in the KCD series, I ended up playing those mini games for hours...
I totally have no idea why, I haven't played any game where the gambling mini games can have 2 different versions with no explanation of why the difference exists...
And if you notice, the Pailune gambling is different than the ones in Hernand and in the desert... Somehow there's no special combo pair cards thing there and you play with 5 cards somehow (like real life poker)...
That was my first time seriously doing it though, I haven't got to the crimson desert region so that den is the biggest den that I found.
I've just won against 3 at the gambling den near Pailune, ended up gaining a little more than 500 silver, and it was on an actual real world 1.5 hours of playtime or just gambling there... Funnily I think it looks several in game days... And my Kliff ended up duelling his last opponent for like 75% of the entire session and TBH I was about to give up at draining his money down to zero but with a lucky last hand I managed to bait the NPC to slowly raise the bet to all in.
I'm sure some of you have done that...
No cheating and no accusation either, just patience and luck needed.

column2: How to Pay Respects in Crimson Desert
column3:
Press L1 to bring up the reticle.

Aim at the casket, then press X to enter Focus Mode.

R3 and □ to place down flowers.

Hold L2 and R3 to pay respects.

column3:
I haven't even geared and invested points into Damiene and Oongka yet. It's just no more enemies are around.

Major issue that many people will run into in another week or so. I'm at 230 hours with the majority of main storyline missions done. As you progress and overtake camps the enemies don't come back. Repeat this until end game and it reaches a point where the map has very minimal things to kill.

There's a sanctum that has a hidden chamber where the chest is located, but in order to get it you need to step on a very specific stone that is on top of a crumbling wall with literally not a single thing indicating you need to do this. L3 is left hand power
R3 is right hand power
R1 or R2 is right hand attack
L1 left hand block (outside combat, focus on what you're looking at)
L2 Bow
X Tap once or twice (not keep tapping) to run
□ interact or jump (you can't interact with something if it doesn't show 'use'. It's not that hard)
Quick menu- hold start (immediately press down and it will take you to the map. Easy)
Those are the basics. Yes there are some funky combos and things could be remapped. Easily I can think of many things I would change. It's far from perfect or intuitive. It's not worth getting a refund.

The ghosting on Kliff’s hand in the first clip is crazy, I hope PA fixes the DLSS issues soon

I don't have DLSS (pre-RDNA4 card) and it's this bad, so it's not a DLSS issue. Or rather, perhaps DLSS doesn't fix it.
I don't know the specifics, but I think the game engine wanted to be "future proof", so it uses a super aggressive denoiser and aggressive sampling of past frames for realtime global illumination which causes tons of ghosting issues. So it has true RTGI which is normally unacceptable performance, but they get around this by shooting 1 ray per 16 pixels (4x4) instead of the gold standard of 1 ray per pixel. In low contrast scenes, you can see the denoiser fail near catastrophically. I hope in a year or two they can patch the game to sample at 1/4 or something so it doesn't look so bad.
Edit: But thinking on it, it's an open world game that has to run on a base PS5. It's not easy to get clean dynamic lighting (no baked lightmaps) everywhere.

Yeah i've seen people say the game use like 16 frames of temporal accumulation for raytracing, which would explain all the ghosting.
I would say that if you need to have it run on base ps5 with good performance and visual quality well you need to use rasterization. It's more work for the developers but I doubt there is a way raytracing is going to look better on a ps5, it's just not built for it.
Plenty of open world have had dynamic lighting with rasterization and while raytracing is supposed to be more accurate, if you have to use all the tricks they did to get acceptable framerate, the visual result become pointless, it just end up looking worse. The only benefit is probably reducing development time.

Took me maybe 2 hours to get used to. I really don't think they're that crazy. Odd, very odd, yes. Rage quit or minimum IQ level necessary, no.
L3 is left hand power
R3 is right hand power
R1 or R2 is right hand attack
L1 left hand block (outside combat, focus on what you're looking at)
L2 Bow
X Tap once or twice (not keep tapping) to run
□ interact or jump (you can't interact with something if it doesn't show 'use'. It's not that hard)
Quick menu- hold start (immediately press down and it will take you to the map. Easy)
Those are the basics. Yes there are some funky combos and things could be remapped. Easily I can think of many things I would change. It's far from perfect or intuitive. It's not worth getting a refund.

The ghosting on Kliff’s hand in the first clip is crazy, I hope PA fixes the DLSS issues soon

I don't have DLSS (pre-RDNA4 card) and it's this bad, so it's not a DLSS issue. Or rather, perhaps DLSS doesn't fix it.
I don't know the specifics, but I think the game engine wanted to be "future proof", so it uses a super aggressive denoiser and aggressive sampling of past frames for realtime global illumination which causes tons of ghosting issues. So it has true RTGI which is normally unacceptable performance, but they get around this by shooting 1 ray per 16 pixels (4x4) instead of the gold standard of 1 ray per pixel. In low contrast scenes, you can see the denoiser fail near catastrophically. I hope in a year or two they can patch the game to sample at 1/4 or something so it doesn't look so bad.
Edit: But thinking on it, it's an open world game that has to run on a base PS5. It's not easy to get clean dynamic lighting (no baked lightmaps) everywhere.

Yeah i've seen people say the game use like 16 frames of temporal accumulation for raytracing, which would explain all the ghosting.
I would say that if you need to have it run on base ps5 with good performance and visual quality well you need to use rasterization. It's more work for the developers but I doubt there is a way raytracing is going to look better on a ps5, it's just not built for it.
Plenty of open world have had dynamic lighting with rasterization and while raytracing is supposed to be more accurate, if you have to use all the tricks they did to get acceptable framerate, the visual result become pointless, it just end up looking worse. The only benefit is probably reducing development time.
